description:
This diagram illustrates the fundamental principles of Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar (InSAR) and Persistent Scatterer Interferometry (PSI) for ground motion detection, composed of three sub-figures:
(a) Shows two satellite passes acquiring Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) images, with radar signals R1 (blue) and R2 (red) returning from a ground target, specifically a Persistent Scatterer (PS) depicted as a house. The differential range, ΔR (red double arrow), represents the change in distance due to ground displacement between the two acquisitions. Trees are marked with red crosses, indicating they are not stable scatterers.
(b) A 3D spatial representation of phase values or displacement, with axes labelled 'slant range' and 'azimuth' for spatial dimensions, and 'baseline' on the vertical axis (values from 0 to 10). The coloured layers represent different phase maps or displacement fields. Several Persistent Scatterer (PS) points and a Reference (Ref) point are indicated within this spatial context, illustrating their distribution and the relative nature of measurements.
(c) A time series diagram showing the temporal evolution of phase values for Persistent Scatterer (PS) and Reference (Ref) points. The Y-axis represents 'time' (from t0 to tN), and the X-axis represents 'space' (along a profile). Black squares denote phase observations for PS and Ref points at different acquisition times (t0, t1, t2, t3, ..., tN). The blue and red curves (α0, α1, α2, α3, ..., αN) illustrate the relative phase changes over time, with red and blue shaded areas indicating displacement in opposing directions. Dashed lines track the temporal deformation of individual PS points, demonstrating how Persistent Scatterer Interferometry measures ground motion time series by comparing the phase history of PS points relative to a stable reference.
